title: Creep feeding piglets before weaning
subtitle: Getting piglets onto solid feed while they still have the sow.
excerpt: Piglets that have encountered solid feed before weaning make the transition off the sow with less weight loss and gut upset. The creep area is a space only piglets can access; the creep feed is high-protein, highly palatable, and introduced small and fresh from around day 10. Most piglets are curious rather than enthusiastic at first.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003). AHDB Pork, sow and litter management guidance, ahdb.org.uk. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg.

glossaryTerms.definition: A section of the farrowing pen with an entrance gap or bar too small for the sow but accessible to piglets. Fitted with a supplementary heat source and creep feed tray. Piglets retreat here to sleep and, eventually, to eat.

content.text: Piglets that have tasted solid feed before weaning show less growth loss at weaning. When sow milk stops, the gut has to switch to a completely new feed. A piglet that has already tried solid food adjusts much faster. The
makes this early introduction possible without competition from the sow.

The creep is a corner of the farrowing pen divided off with a board at 20 to 25 cm height, high enough to block the sow but easily stepped over by piglets from day five. The farrowing lamp hangs above this area from the start; piglets are drawn to the heat and begin sleeping there within the first 48 hours of life. The lamp and creep area must be set up before farrowing.

Start offering creep feed from around day 10 using a low-sided tray or flat pan. Place a very small amount (a handful per litter) directly in the sleeping area. Highly palatable creep pellets formulated specifically for pre-weaning pigs are more likely to be eaten than standard weaners' pellets. Remove uneaten feed after 12 hours and replace with fresh; stale or damp feed is ignored.

Most piglets investigate creep feed out of curiosity by day 14 and begin eating consistently by day 21. Intake increases rapidly in the final week before weaning as they spend more time at the tray and less time suckling. By the target weaning day (typically 28 days in a smallholding setting) a well-managed litter should be consuming 50 to 100 g of creep per piglet per day.

Remove the sow, leave the piglets in the familiar farrowing pen for the first two to three days post-weaning to reduce environmental stress. Continue the same creep feed for one week before transitioning to standard weaners' pellets. A weaned piglet that has been eating creep feed will look for the tray as soon as the sow goes; a piglet that has had no creep exposure may go off feed for two to three days.
